text: The surgeon begins the preperitoneal repair by working through the fat around the falciform ligament, carefully developing the initial incision into the peritoneum while avoiding entry into the retromuscular space. Visible scarring from previous procedures is noted.
The surgeon resumes dissection in areas with abundant fat to avoid creating peritoneal holes, aiming to develop a large peritoneal pocket for mesh placement to address a two-centimeter defect.

The surgeon uses an 11-centimeter circular mesh to repair the hernia, creating a sizable pocket superiorly. The dissection proceeds around the hernia inferiorly, facilitated by the presence of abundant preperitoneal fat, which simplifies the procedure.
The surgeon approaches a structure that initially appears to be the hernia but is likely related to the five millimeter trocar used for the patient's VP shunt.

The surgeon identifies the actual hernia and begins the reduction process, manipulating the hernia sac without bedside assistance. The surgical field shows mostly pre-peritoneal fat surrounding the hernia.

The surgeon reduces the incarcerated hernia and begins taking down adhesions to create a clear working space. The next step involves developing a pocket for the placement of an eleven by eleven centimeter mesh.

The surgeon encounters peri-umbilical vessels that are prone to bleeding during the robotic hernia repair. The robotic approach allows for clear visualization and management of these vessels, which might be challenging to control with an open technique.

The surgeon develops the contralateral peritoneal flap, encountering difficulty but eventually progressing. The transversalis fascia is kept elevated while the peritoneum is pushed downward. There is visible oozing around the vessels, which is controlled using COAG and bipolar instruments.
The surgeon continues to develop a broad pocket around the hernia defect, aiming for at least four to five centimeters of circumferential space to ensure adequate mesh coverage.

The surgeon continues to develop the space for mesh placement, improving visualization of the surgical field. The majority of the pocket development is completed, and the surgeon begins closing the larger defect transversely, using a suture technique that reverses to lock the suture. The closure of the smaller defect will be integrated with the same suture.

The surgeon sutures transversely from the contralateral to the ipsilateral side, with assistance from the scrub tech to reduce the hernia and umbilical skin, incorporating it into an umbilicalplasty. The fascia is being tightened.

The surgeon uses a permanent V-lock suture to close a smaller defect, integrating it into the closure of another smaller hole. The closure is performed in a vertical orientation, contrasting with the transverse orientation used for the umbilicus. The suture is locked by reversing it for two to three bites.
The surgeon places a Bard soft mesh over the closed defects. The mesh appears slightly large, prompting the surgeon to enlarge the pocket for a flat fit.

The surgeon positions the barred soft mesh with the sticky side facing upwards in the ventral hernia repair, ensuring initial fixation. Additional fixation points will be applied to secure the mesh in place.
The surgeon performs central fixation of the mesh at two or three spots without peripheral fixation, ensuring the mesh is not overly tightened to prevent deformation.
The surgeon ensures the mesh is securely integrated by adjusting it to prevent movement beyond a few millimeters, preparing for a flat closure of the surgical site.
The surgeon ties three knots using the remaining suture to close the flap and reduce the hernia, completing the procedure without needing a new suture.
